did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning algorithms (Classification Tree, Random Forest, CN2, Naïve Bayes, k Nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Machine) combined with feature selection methods (ReliefF, Information Gain, Gain Ratio, Gini Index, SVM weight, RandomForest) to classify melanocytic skin lesions (benign, suspicious, malignant) in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from applying feature selection methods and machine learning algorithms (Classification Tree, Random Forest, CN2, Naïve Bayes, k Nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Machine) to a medical dataset of 548 cases to improve melanoma diagnosis accuracy. It includes specific methodology, dataset description, experimental procedures (10-fold cross-validation), and quantitative results (Table II) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Medical diagnosis support and accuracy improvement by application of total scoring from feature selection approach
journal: Proceedings of the Federated Conference on Computer Science and Information Systems
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve melanoma diagnosis by applying feature selection methods to identify the most relevant attributes from the ABCD dermoscopy formula. Using a dataset of 548 melanocytic skin lesions (benign, suspicious, and malignant), six feature ranking measures were employed to select core features and construct a new Total Dermatoscopy Score (NewTDS). Six machine learning algorithms, including Support Vector Machine and Random Forest, were evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation across various metrics. The results demonstrated that adding the NewTDS to a reduced feature set significantly enhanced prediction performance, achieving an average AUC of 0.99 and an Informadness score of 0.98. The research concludes that this approach effectively reduces dimensionality while improving diagnostic accuracy for melanocytic lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant melanocytic lesions; 114 cases in the dataset; used as the positive class for ROC curve analysis.
Benign and suspicious melanocytic lesions; 326 cases of Benign/Blue nevus and 108 cases of Suspicious nevus in the dataset.
The broad category of lesions studied, comprising the 548 total cases (benign, suspicious, and malignant).

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of melanocytic skin lesions (benign, suspicious, or malignant)
all_task_types.details: The paper applies six machine learning algorithms (Classification Tree, Random Forest, CN2, Naïve Bayes, k Nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Machine) to classify melanocytic skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories: benign, suspicious (borderline), and malignant. The task is explicitly defined as classification, with performance evaluated using classification metrics such as Classification Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Melanocytic skin lesions dataset
datasets_used.description: A medical dataset consisting of 548 cases of melanocytic skin lesions used for diagnosis support. The dataset includes descriptive attributes based on the ABCD dermoscopy formula (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diversity of structures). It was used to evaluate feature selection methods and machine learning algorithms for classifying lesions.

use.training: Used for training machine learning models (Classification Tree, Random Forest, CN2, Naïve Bayes, k Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Machine) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Used to calculate feature importance using six ranking measures (ReliefF, Information Gain, Gain Ratio, Gini Index, SVM weight, RandomForest) and to generate contrast variables (shadow variables) for specificity testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of six machine learning algorithms (Classification Tree, Random Forest, CN2, Naïve Bayes, k Nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Machine) on the dataset.

To estimate the expected level of false discovery in feature selection by duplicating original variables and randomly permuting their values, then comparing the selection frequency of these non-informative variables against original features.

To rank feature importance based on the frequency of presence of different features in rule models and the quality of those rules, serving as a threshold to separate core attributes from less informative ones.

statistical_methods_used.method: Classification Accuracy (CA), Sensitivity, Specificity, Area Under ROC curve (AUC), Information Score (IS), F1 score (F1), Precision, Brier measure, Matthew Coefficient Correlation (MCC), Informadness ratio
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively compare and evaluate the prediction performance of the different machine learning models across various feature combinations.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It used contrast variables (shadow variables) to estimate the expected level of false discovery in feature selection and compared feature importance scores against a threshold derived from these contrast variables, but did not perform statistical significance testing on the classification results.

feature_combinations: Selected core feature set (6 attributes: Border, Asymmetry, Pigment network, Blue color, Branched streaks, White color)
All original feature set with TDS parameter
Selected core feature set with NewTDS parameter
Melanocytic skin lesions dataset (548 ca.notes: The addition of the NewTDS parameter to the selected core feature set resulted in the highest performance, with Average AUC and Specificity reaching 1.00, and Average Informadness reaching 0.98. The core feature set alone showed slightly lower performance compared to the full feature set (e.g., Average AUC dropped from 0.95 to 0.93). The standard TDS parameter improved performance over the base sets but was generally outperformed by the NewTDS parameter.

architecture_used.main_model: Six machine learning algorithms: Classification Tree (CT), Random Forest (RF), CN2 decision rules algorithm (CN2), Naïve Bayes (NB), k Nearest Neighbors (kNN), and Support Vector Machine (SVM).
architecture_used.input_size: 13 descriptive attributes based on the ABCD dermoscopy formula (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diversity of structures). A new feature 'NewTDS' was added to the dataset, resulting in 14 features for specific experiments.

architecture_details.new_feature_formula: NewTDS = 38.72 * Border + 31.82 * Asymmetry + 23.22 * PigmentNetwork + 20.00 * BlueColor + 16.60 * BranchedStreaks + 15.50 * WhiteColor
architecture_details.contrast_variables: Shadow variables created by duplicating original variables and randomly permuting their values to estimate false discovery rate.

preprocessing_techniques: Feature selection using ranking measures (ReliefF, Information Gain, Gain Ratio, Gini Index, SVM weight, RandomForest, RuleQualityFS) to identify core attributes
Creation of contrast variables (shadow variables) by duplicating original variables and randomly permuting their values to estimate false discovery rate
Calculation of a new composite feature (NewTDS) based on selected core features and their importance weights

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for feature selection validation rather than training augmentation. Original variables were duplicated and their values randomly permuted across all objects to create non-informative shadow variables. These were used to estimate the expected level of false discovery in feature selection by comparing selection frequency against original features.

conclusions: The study successfully identified a core set of six features (Border, Asymmetry, Pigment network, Blue color, Branched streaks, and White color) as the most relevant attributes for classifying melanocytic skin lesions using the RuleQualityFS measure.

The introduction of a new Total Dermatoscopy Score (NewTDS) formula, calculated using weights derived from the RuleQualityFS measure, significantly improved diagnostic accuracy compared to the standard TDS and base feature sets.

Adding the NewTDS parameter to the selected core feature set resulted in the highest prediction performance, achieving an average Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 1.00 and an average Informadness score of 0.98 across six machine learning algorithms.

The proposed method effectively reduces the dimensionality of the problem by focusing on a minimal set of important features while simultaneously improving the complexity and accuracy of melanoma diagnosis.
The use of contrast variables (shadow variables) allowed for the estimation of the false discovery rate, establishing a threshold that distinguished core features from less informative ones.

Among the six tested machine learning algorithms (Classification Tree, Random Forest, CN2, Naïve Bayes, k Nearest Neighbors, and Support Vector Machine), the combination of the core feature set with NewTDS yielded superior results, with Random Forest, CN2, and SVM showing particularly high performance metrics.
important_remarks: The study uses a small, single-institution dataset of only 548 cases, which limits statistical power and generalizability.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using 10-fold cross-validation on the internal dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and data leakage risks.

The reported performance metrics are exceptionally high (e.g., Average AUC of 1.00 and Specificity of 1.00 for the NewTDS model), which is unusually strong for medical diagnosis tasks and may indicate optimistic bias due to the small sample size and lack of independent testing.

The feature selection process utilized 'contrast variables' (shadow variables) to determine a threshold for feature importance, but this method does not replace rigorous statistical significance testing or external validation.

The dataset composition is imbalanced, with 326 benign/Blue nevi, 108 suspicious nevi, and 114 malignant melanomas, which may bias the classifier towards the majority class despite the high specificity scores.
The research is a conference paper (2015) with limited scope, focusing on algorithmic feature selection rather than clinical deployment or real-world dermatological validation.
